interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Civil Rights Activist; former Secretary of the Terrell County Movement
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Ohio State University and Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

main_summary.summary:
Johnnie Ruth Brawner McCullar, born February 4, 1940, in Sasser, Terrell County, Georgia, describes her upbringing on a farm and her family's eventual move into the town of Sasser in the 1950s. The daughter of Clifford and Johnnie Lou Brawner, she attended a one-room rural elementary school called Panty Grove before transferring to Dunbar Elementary in Sasser and then Carver High School in Dawson, Georgia, where the segregated facilities lacked adequate heating, books with intact pages, and lunch facilities. McCullar enrolled at Albany State in 1960, intending to become a teacher, but left in 1962 after recurring illness she attributed to the dormitory food. While at Albany State, she participated in one civil rights march downtown that involved a fellow student named Bertha Gober.

Returning home in 1962, McCullar joined what she calls the Terrell Movement (the Terrell County movement), under the presidency of Lucius Holloway, and became its secretary. Mass meetings convened first at Mount Olive Baptist Church and later at her own family home. She names SNCC field workers she encountered through the movement, including Charles Sherrod, Don Stewart, Prathia Hall, and Ralph Allen, and mentions that Carolyn Daniels housed SNCC workers in the area. She and her parents attempted to register to vote at the county courthouse, where the registrar was often absent and required applicants to read a scripture from the Bible before completing the form. After they registered, her mother lost her cook's job at a white school. McCullar describes canvassing door-to-door with high school students Gladys Clark and Anadine Clark, the fear of residents that talking to canvassers would cost them their jobs or rental homes, and the ongoing harassment of mass meetings by Sheriff Z.T. Mathews and his deputies.

In August 1962, Mount Olive Baptist Church was burned in the middle of the night. McCullar describes the loss as feeling like a death and reports that subsequent meetings were held first at the Methodist Church and then under a tent erected behind the rebuilt church's foundation. She participated in sit-ins led by Reverend Samuel Wells at a Dawson restaurant, served as a poll watcher in local and national elections, and helped run a literacy school that taught adults to read and write so they could register to vote. She recalls testimony she and her family gave to U.S. Department of Justice investigators and federal court appearances in Americus.

McCullar married in 1967, moved briefly to Topeka, Kansas, then to Atlanta for four years, before returning to Albany to work at Phoebe Putney Memorial Hospital. She resumed civil rights work through the Terrell County NAACP, serving as a deputy registrar from approximately 1979 until 1996, when she stopped because expanded registration laws meant people could register anywhere. She notes that the local NAACP branch president, JL Barnes, was murdered in 1994 and that she believes his activism contributed to his death. She closes with an exhortation to vote, hold elected officials accountable, and persist despite the persistence of racial inequality.
key_themes: Voter Registration in the Deep South
Terrell County Movement and SNCC Southwest Georgia Project
Long-Term Civic Engagement and the NAACP
main_summary.historical_significance: McCullar's testimony documents the grassroots Terrell County Movement of 1962, including the burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church in Sasser, from the vantage point of a young woman who served as the local movement's secretary and lived through Sheriff Z.T. Mathews's intimidation campaign firsthand. Her account corroborates the canonical SNCC Southwest Georgia Project history through naming Charles Sherrod, Prathia Hall, and other field organizers, while also adding texture about local Black families' decision-making, the role of Lucius Holloway and Carolyn Daniels, and the long arc of voter-registration work that continued into the 1990s. The interview is especially valuable as a representative voice of rural Black Georgians who sustained the movement after national press attention waned.

improvements: Could more explicitly note that McCullar's mother's job loss was at a white school where she had worked as a cook (cross-verifiable detail from earlier in transcript).
Could clarify that the speaker says her family meetings moved 'to my home' after the Zion Hill / Sardis Baptist church meetings.

chapters.title: Birth and Family Origins in Sasser, Terrell County
chapters.summary: McCullar identifies herself as Johnnie Ruth Brawner McCullar, born February 4, 1940, in Sasser, Terrell County, Georgia. She names her parents as Clifford Brawner and Johnnie Lou Brawner, both of Southwest Georgia origin. Her father's parents moved from Worth County, which sits near the Terrell County line, and her mother's family she understands originated in some part of Alabama. The chapter opens with interviewer Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries introducing the setting at Albany State University on March 9, 2013, on behalf of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
related_events: Library of Congress Civil Rights History Project
Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

chapters.title: Farm Life and Move to Sasser
chapters.summary: McCullar describes her parents' work ethic: her mother was always working, canning, sewing, and making quilts; her father was first a farmer, then worked at the Marine Base, and finally retired after about 28 years at a fertilizer plant in Albany, Georgia (she recalls the name Swift). The family moved off the farm in the 1950s. She frankly states she did not like life on the farm, citing fear of animals, heat, mosquitoes, gnats, and especially snakes. The farm was located "down to four-shore" near a place she calls Dubgaate, off Old Dawson Road. When the family moved to Sasser, she preferred it largely because there were fewer snakes and no farm work. Her chores on the farm had included feeding chickens, milking the cow (which she feared), and occasionally picking cotton, which she did not enjoy.
related_events: Family migration off farm in the 1950s

improvements: Place name 'Dubgaate' is McCullar's pronunciation; canonical spelling unverified.

chapters.title: Segregated Schooling: Panty Grove, Dunbar, and Carver High
chapters.summary: McCullar describes her segregated education. She first attended Panty Grove, a one-room school with seven grades, one teacher (her first teacher was Vanny Wakefield), and approximately 40 students. After sixth grade she transferred to Dunbar Elementary in Sasser, which housed first through ninth grade with one grade per classroom. She then went to Carver High School in Dawson, beginning in 1955 or 1956. She describes Carver as cold, with only one heater per classroom and students wearing heavy coats indoors, no lunchroom, and a divided auditorium where students could hear what was happening in the other classroom. Although the famous Brown ruling had passed two years earlier, she says there was no discussion of desegregating schools because people seemed afraid to discuss such matters and Black teachers were told what to teach. Her parents, while not knowledgeable about segregation policy, recognized that students lacked what they needed: books with pages torn out passed down from the white schools, often only one spelling book per class, and the requirement that students themselves clean the buildings and lay down dust compound.

improvements: Spellings 'Panty Grove' and 'Vanny Wakefield' are speaker's pronunciations; canonical forms not independently verified.

chapters.title: Year Out of School and Enrollment at Albany State
chapters.summary: After graduating from Carver High School in 1959, McCullar had to wait a year before college because her older sister was in nursing school in Bibb County, Macon, Georgia, and the family could only support one tuition at a time. During that year she babysat in the neighborhood and kept house while her mother worked as a cook at the high school. Once her sister finished, McCullar enrolled at Albany State in 1960. She chose Albany State partly because her parents, knowing she had been a sickly child, did not want her to go far from home. She entered intending to become a teacher (a path her parents prescribed because a former instructor had said she needed it) and chose science as her major, even though she admits she was not particularly good at it.
related_events: Albany State College / Albany State University
Higher education for Black women in Georgia (1960)

chapters.title: First Civil Rights March at Albany State and Illness
chapters.summary: McCullar describes living on the Albany State campus, enjoying the experience overall, but contracting hepatitis from the campus food, which she and other students complained about repeatedly to the administration without effect. She reports participating in one downtown march while at Albany State, which she remembers was associated with a young woman named Bertha Gober, a fellow Albany State student. She describes the march as scary because she did not know what would happen downtown, and recalls fearing her parents' reaction. Her parents, however, did not get mad when they found out; she says by then things had started 'opening up.' The head cook at the white school where McCullar's mother worked mentioned the march to her mother, expressing surprise that her daughter would do such a thing. McCullar did not participate in any further marches while still on campus. In spring 1962, her illness from the campus food forced her to leave Albany State.

improvements: McCullar says only 'a young lady named Bertha Gober' and does not specify when the march occurred or what role Gober played; summary should not infer beyond what the transcript states.

chapters.title: Joining the Terrell Movement and Voter Registration
chapters.summary: After returning home in 1962, McCullar found her parents already involved in what she calls the Terrell Movement, attending meetings first at Zion Hill or Sardis Baptist Church in Dawson and then at her own family home. The organization had begun forming around 1960, with Lucius Holloway as its president. Although she does not recall being formally elected, she became secretary, taking notes at mass meetings where SNCC workers including Charles Sherrod, Don Stewart, Prathia Hall, and Ralph Allen would speak. She and her parents attempted to register to vote; the white county registrar would frequently leave the office (he was also Santa Claus at the post office at Christmas) and required Black applicants to read a scripture from the Bible before completing the form. After successfully registering, her mother lost her cook's job at the white school. A subsequent employer in Albany, where her mother did housework, was called and asked to fire her but refused, saying she only needed her to do her job.
related_events: Reprisal firings of Black voter registrants

improvements: Name 'Don Stewart' is rendered as 'Don Steward' in transcript; the canonical Albany / SNCC SW Georgia organizer Don Stewart is the most plausible referent but not independently confirmed in ground truth.

chapters.title: Mass Meetings, Sheriff Mathews, and Canvassing
chapters.summary: McCullar describes Wednesday-night mass meetings of about 30 to 40 people held at Mount Olive Baptist Church in Terrell County, with neighboring counties supporting each other's meeting nights. Sheriff Z.T. Mathews and his deputies would arrive at the meetings, put their feet on the seats, and speak 'ugly,' insisting that Black residents were happy with how things were and that 'outsiders' were stirring them up. McCullar identifies Charles Sherrod as the first SNCC worker she met, describing him as sweet, calm, and self-assured, and notes that other SNCC workers, including a woman named Carolyn Daniels, hosted them in her home. She describes canvassing door-to-door with high school students Gladys Clark (now deceased) and her sister Anadine Clark; residents called them 'Freedom Riders' and many ran inside, afraid of losing their jobs or rental housing if seen speaking with them. McCullar also describes participating in sit-ins led by Reverend Samuel Wells at a Dawson restaurant, where he carefully instructed participants to leave if confronted, to avoid anyone getting hurt.
related_events: Mount Olive Baptist Church mass meetings

chapters.title: Burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church and Aftermath
chapters.summary: McCullar describes the August 1962 burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church (her family church) as feeling 'like a death.' Done in the middle of the night, the perpetrators were never identified, though local Black residents believed white people had done it. The mass meetings moved temporarily to the Methodist Church, but that congregation grew afraid their own church would be burned and asked the movement to leave. Once the foundation of the rebuilt Mount Olive had been laid, participants put chairs on it for meetings until that was prohibited, after which a large tent was erected behind the foundation for both Sunday worship and movement meetings. McCullar's brother helped build the foundation. Following the burning, her family took turns sitting up at night watching for suspicious cars approaching the home, ready to evacuate the family if anyone tried to set a fire. Her father expressed unprintable anger; the entire community felt the loss. After the church burning, McCullar also describes an adult literacy school (a 'dope school' in her speech, likely an adult or 'open' school) where local activists taught Black adults to read and write their names so they could register to vote.
related_events: Mount Olive Baptist Church burning, Sasser, August 1962

improvements: McCullar's 'dope school' phrase appears to be a transcription artifact for 'adult school' or 'opportunity school'; treated cautiously as literacy school in summary.

chapters.title: Federal Investigations, Poll Watching, and Awakening to Segregation
chapters.summary: McCullar describes the investigation that followed the church burning. The Georgia Bureau of Investigation came first, which she believes was largely to cover for local authorities. The U.S. Department of Justice followed, taking statements from movement participants. Investigators took her small notebook of meeting notes back to Washington; she never received it back. People from Sasser had to travel to federal court in Americus to testify about the church burnings and an incident in which a local police officer shot at SNCC workers. McCullar describes her own emerging awareness of segregation: she had been protected by her parents from white-folks' workplaces growing up, so her sharpest encounters with prejudice came through movement work, when she realized she could not sit at a restaurant counter and had to buy a hamburger 'through the hole in the wall.' She also served as a poll watcher in local and national elections, helping illiterate voters who had to sign a form to receive assistance, and reports that federal observers were present at the polls.
related_events: U.S. Department of Justice civil rights investigations
Federal court testimony in Americus, GA
Shooting at SNCC workers in Terrell County

chapters.title: Marriage, Move to Kansas, and Return to Albany
chapters.summary: McCullar describes how movement activity gradually slowed once Black residents had been registered. Her direct involvement tapered off when her parents brought her home to care for her sister's newborn child. She married in 1967 and moved to Topeka, Kansas, which she calls a wonderful experience that allowed her to see other places. After Topeka she lived in Atlanta for four years before returning to Albany to work at Phoebe Putney Memorial Hospital in the Central Sterile Supply / CNA function. On her return she found segregation signs (the 'black water fountain' and 'white water fountain' markers) gone, restaurants and theaters open to all, and the schools in Terrell County integrated, with everyone riding the school bus.
related_events: Marriage and relocation to Topeka, Kansas (1967)
Post-1965 school integration in Terrell County

chapters.title: NAACP, Deputy Registrar, and Reflections on Lasting Inequality
chapters.summary: After returning to the area, McCullar joined the Terrell County NAACP and became a deputy registrar in 1979, serving in that role until approximately 1996 when expanded voter-registration laws allowed people to register anywhere. She would spend four hours each Saturday morning registering voters. The branch president of the local NAACP, JL Barnes, was murdered in 1994; McCullar believes his activism contributed to his death. She reflects on the limits of progress: while things appear better on the surface, there remains, in her view, much that is 'stuffed under the cover,' and people continue to work to prevent Black Americans from reaching what she calls 'where God intended for us to be.' She points to opposition to the Black president (Barack Obama, at the time of the 2013 interview) as evidence that such resistance continues. She closes with an exhortation for Black Americans to register, vote, support and hold accountable their elected officials.

improvements: Transcript renders the deputy-registrar exit year as a range tied to a vague 'whenever' clause about expanded registration; summary should not anchor to a specific federal statute the speaker does not name.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Civil Rights Activist; former Secretary of the Terrell County Movement
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Ohio State University and Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: I was born in Terrell County, in Sasser, Georgia, Georgia. (Segment 9, 00:01:09,000)
Returning home in 1962, McCullar describes her parents' meetings: 'When I got home, my parents were already involved.' (Segment 343, 00:20:59,000)
Then later on I got married and I moved away. (Segment 895, 00:52:32,880)
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interview follows a clear chronological arc from birth and family, through schooling, college, movement work, marriage, and post-movement civic engagement. Transitions are interviewer-driven but the resulting structure is coherent. Some sections are choppy because McCullar gives brief answers to many questions.

most_vivid_moment.quote: If you went to get a hamburger, you had to get it through the hole in the wall because you couldn't go inside.

evidence: Carver High School in Dawson, with one heater per classroom and divided auditorium (Segments 108-119, 00:07:53 - 00:08:50)
Mass meetings of 30 to 40 people on Wednesday nights at Mount Olive Baptist Church (Segments 468-481, 00:27:50 - 00:28:25)
Sheriff Z.T. Mathews 'would put their feet on the seats and just talk be ugly' (Segment 459-461, 00:27:19)
vivid_details_examples.justification: McCullar provides several vivid concrete moments — the hand-me-down books with pages torn out, the hole-in-the-wall hamburger window, Sheriff Mathews's feet on the seats — but many answers are brief and lack sensory detail. Specific names of SNCC workers, fellow canvassers, and her family members are provided, but dates are often approximate.

most_authentic_moment.quote: It was like a death that someone you love had died... How could you burn a house of God? Because people will want to register to vote.

vulnerability_elements: Naming her own fear during the downtown march
Naming her family's loss of her mother's job for registering
Naming her sense that systemic resistance continues into 2013
emotional_authenticity.justification: McCullar speaks with notable emotional clarity about the church burning, the firing of her mother, and her ongoing pessimism about racial progress under the Obama administration. She is not performatively emotive but consistently honest about fear, loss, and frustration.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Narrative quality is solid: a clear chronological arc with consistent emotional truth-telling. The interview format yields short, direct answers rather than extended storytelling, which limits structural drama and sensory richness but does not undercut authenticity. McCullar's accounts of the church burning and her mother's firing carry real weight.

major_events: Burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church, Sasser, August 1962 (Segments 699-713, 00:36:00 - 00:39:42)
Sheriff Z.T. Mathews's intimidation of mass meetings (Segments 457-491, 00:27:00 - 00:29:00)
Federal court hearings in Americus regarding the church burnings and shooting of SNCC workers (Segments 824-830, 00:46:57 - 00:47:30)

key_figures.name: Charles Sherrod
key_figures.relationship: SNCC organizer she encountered through the Terrell Movement

key_figures.name: Prathia Hall
key_figures.relationship: SNCC field secretary who spoke at Terrell County mass meetings

key_figures.name: Lucius Holloway
key_figures.relationship: President of the Terrell County Movement; she was its secretary

key_figures.name: Reverend Samuel Wells
key_figures.relationship: Led sit-ins she participated in at Dawson restaurant

key_figures.name: Carolyn Daniels
key_figures.relationship: Host to SNCC workers in the area

key_figures.name: Sheriff Z.T. Mathews
key_figures.relationship: Sheriff who harassed mass meetings she attended

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: McCullar served as secretary of a local SW Georgia movement organization at the apex of SNCC's Southwest Georgia Project; she has insider familiarity with SNCC field workers, with Sheriff Mathews's intimidation tactics, and with how Black families decided whether to risk attempted registration.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: As secretary of the Terrell County Movement and a participant in the Mount Olive Baptist Church meetings, McCullar offers genuinely close firsthand access to canonical SW Georgia Movement events including the Mt. Olive burning and Sheriff Mathews's intimidation campaign. She knew the SNCC organizers personally.

precision_examples: Born February 4, 1940, in Sasser, Terrell County (Segments 9-10, 00:01:09 - 00:01:18)
Parents Clifford Brawner and Johnnie Lou Brawner (Segment 12, 00:01:20)
First teacher Vanny Wakefield at Panty Grove (Segment 81, 00:06:22)
Canvassing partners Gladys Clark and Anadine Clark (Segments 528-529, 00:30:47 - 00:30:58)
Mount Olive Baptist Church burned in August 1962, while she was attending and her brother helped rebuild the foundation (Segments 643-647, 738; 00:36:23, 00:41:54)
verifiable_claims: Mount Olive Baptist Church burning, Sasser, summer 1962
Sheriff Z.T. Mathews's intimidation of mass meetings
Lucius Holloway as president of the Terrell County Movement
Charles Sherrod, Prathia Hall, Ralph Allen as SNCC SW Georgia organizers
Carolyn Daniels as host of SNCC workers in Terrell County
specificity_of_details.justification: McCullar provides numerous full names, specific dates of birth and entry to college, and identifiable place names. Some events lack precise dates and a few names (the head cook, the lady who refused to fire her mother) are unrecalled, but the verifiable backbone is strong.

new_information: Local mechanics of the Terrell County Movement, including the rotation of meeting nights across nearby counties and the role of secretary
Detail on Lucius Holloway as movement president and Carolyn Daniels as SNCC host (often overshadowed by Sherrod in the canonical literature)
Long arc of voter registration work continuing into the 1990s through the Terrell County NAACP
understudied_aspects: Rural Black women's leadership in the post-Albany Movement local organizing
Continuity between 1960s movement work and post-Movement civic engagement
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Complicates the standard narrative that Movement activity ended after Albany failed, by documenting ongoing voter-registration and NAACP work through 1996; also adds texture to the 'Terrible Terrell' shorthand by showing how local Black families navigated the period.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Useful corroborative testimony for historians of SNCC's SW Georgia Project, the Mt. Olive burning, and the longer arc of Black voter mobilization in rural southwest Georgia.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: McCullar's testimony adds significant value to the corpus of Terrell County / SW Georgia Movement oral history. She corroborates and adds texture to known events while contributing a fuller account of post-Movement civic continuity.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Historical value is high. McCullar offers insider perspective on canonical SW Georgia Movement events including the Mount Olive Baptist Church burning, names the canonical organizers (Sherrod, Hall, Allen), and traces a long arc of activism from 1962 into the late 1990s. The interview corroborates and complements the better-documented Albany Movement record.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: McCullar describes growing from a girl protected by her parents from direct experience of segregation, who saw segregated facilities as 'the way it counted, the way it was supposed to be,' to a movement secretary and canvasser who recognized racial injustice and committed to fighting it for Black and white alike.
key_turning_points: Joining the Terrell Movement on return from Albany State (Segment 343, 00:20:59)
The burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church (Segments 643-713, 00:36:23 - 00:39:42)
Realizing through movement work that she had been excluded from spaces she had thought were 'separate but equal' (Segments 865-879, 00:50:25 - 00:51:25)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Before movement work, McCullar saw segregation as part of an accepted order; after movement work, she committed to lifelong voter-registration and NAACP activism.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Her perseverance from 1962 movement work into 1990s NAACP voter-registration work models long-haul citizenship.
transformation_growth.justification: There is a real transformation arc from sheltered child to lifelong organizer, articulated explicitly in the segments where she describes 'seeing now what I've been missing.' The transformation is genuine but not framed dramatically; it is more about commitment than identity rupture.

peak_moments.description: Burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church and McCullar likening it to a death
peak_moments.stakes: Loss of the central organizing space; symbolic attack on the community's faith and movement

peak_moments.description: Family taking turns watching at night for arsonists after the burning
peak_moments.stakes: Family safety; risk of being burned in their own home

peak_moments.description: First downtown march at Albany State, fear of the unknown
peak_moments.stakes: Possible arrest, parental disapproval, physical danger

conflict_types: Generational fear and persuasion of neighbors
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From quiet fear and grief to determination and hope
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Several genuine high-stakes moments are present (the church burning, watching for arsonists, federal court testimony) but they are told quietly rather than dramatized. The interview lacks sustained suspense or triumphant peaks; instead the dramatic content accumulates slowly.

universal_themes: Fear of the unknown when taking a stand
Loss of a beloved place (the church)
relatable_struggles: A mother losing her job for her conscience
A child realizing the world is not what they thought
A community persisting after a major loss
human_moments: Adults rejoicing at learning to write their names at the literacy school (Segments 662-668, 00:32:00 - 00:37:42)
Mother making cakes for SNCC workers (Segments 518-519, 00:30:00)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Audiences of all backgrounds can relate to the family-centered character of her account, the cost of standing up for one's rights, and the experience of long-haul civic commitment.
relatable_experiences.justification: McCullar's family-centered framing and her quiet persistence make her experience legible across audiences. Some moments — the church burning, the adult literacy class — translate especially well to general listeners.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Emotional resonance is solid but not overwhelming. The dramatic content is real but understated, and the transformation arc is one of committed continuity rather than identity rupture. Universal themes of family, fear, and persistence land effectively.

barriers: Some passages are difficult to follow due to transcription artifacts (e.g., 'dope school' for adult/opportunity school)
Local place names (Dubgaate, Panty Grove) are not always self-explanatory
Speaker uses brief, fragmentary answers throughout
language_clarity.justification: McCullar's speech is clear and free of movement jargon, but the transcript contains transcription artifacts that make a few passages ambiguous and the speaker's tendency toward short answers can leave context unstated.

high_energy_sections: Burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church and aftermath (Segments 643-765, 00:36:23 - 00:43:38)
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Best in the middle third covering movement work; first and last quarters can drift due to brevity of answers.
narrative_flow.justification: The interviewer guides the chronology effectively and the central section about Movement work carries strong momentum. The opening biographical Q&A and the closing reflections feel slower because answers are clipped.

themes_connected: Importance of civic participation across generations
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: McCullar's closing reflections on the Obama presidency and on the continued need to register, vote, and hold officials accountable directly link 1960s movement work to 2013 audiences.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: She frequently makes the 'so what' explicit, exhorting listeners to follow her example.
broader_themes_connection.justification: The interview ends with a strong, explicit linkage between her movement work, the long arc of voter registration, and the contemporary moment, including her view on the Obama presidency and the limits of progress.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Accessibility is good overall. Clarity is moderate due to dialect and transcription artifacts, pacing is uneven across the chronological arc, but the explicit connection McCullar draws to contemporary civic responsibility makes the interview broadly meaningful.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is a valuable Civil Rights History Project interview that documents the Terrell County Movement of 1962 from the position of its secretary. McCullar offers insider perspective on the burning of Mount Olive Baptist Church in Sasser, the intimidation campaign of Sheriff Z.T. Mathews, and the work of SNCC's Southwest Georgia Project organizers Charles Sherrod, Prathia Hall, Don Stewart, and Ralph Allen. Her account adds important texture by tracing the long arc of voter-registration work from 1962 through approximately 1996 via the Terrell County NAACP. Narrative quality is solid, emotional content is honest if understated, and broader thematic connections to contemporary politics are explicitly made.
key_strengths: Insider firsthand perspective on canonical SW Georgia Movement events including the Mount Olive Baptist Church burning
Long temporal arc from 1962 movement work through 1990s NAACP voter registration, useful for historians of post-Albany Movement continuity
Honest emotional reckoning with both the period's losses and the persistence of structural racism into 2013
key_limitations: Short, often fragmentary answers throughout limit narrative momentum and sensory richness; the interview format yields a question-and-answer rhythm rather than extended storytelling
Transcription artifacts ('dope school,' 'Dubgaate,' 'Panty Grove,' 'Don Steward') introduce some ambiguity for downstream researchers

claims.claim: Johnnie Ruth Brawner McCullar was born February 4, 1940, in Sasser, Terrell County, Georgia.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was born in Terrell County, in Sasser, Georgia, Georgia. (Segment 9) / And you were born February 4, 1940. (Segment 10)
claims.rationale: Birth date and location confirmed in interviewer's stated reading and McCullar's own opening answer.

claims.claim: Her parents were Clifford Brawner and Johnnie Lou Brawner.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Clifford Brawner and Johnnie Lou Brawner. (Segment 12)
claims.rationale: Direct answer to interviewer's question about her parents' names.

claims.claim: Her father's parents moved from Worth County to the area; her mother's family originated from Alabama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My father's parents moved here from Worth County, which is a few miles from Terrell County... My mother, I don't quite know. I understand from her that her family originated from some parts of Alabama. (Segments 16-17)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: The family moved off the farm in the 1950s.
claims.supporting_excerpt: We moved off the farm in the 50s. I don't remember exactly what year. (Segment 23)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: Her father worked at the Marine Base and then at a fertilizer plant (Swift) in Albany, retiring after about 28 years.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He worked for a while at the Marine Base. And from there to the... fertilizer plant, I can't remember the name Swift. That's the name of... / And Albany, Georgia, yes. He retired from there after about 28 years. (Segments 24-27)
claims.rationale: All employment claims sourced to McCullar's direct testimony.

claims.claim: She attended Panty Grove, a one-room school with seven grades, about 40 students, and a first teacher named Vanny Wakefield.
claims.supporting_excerpt: One room school. The name of it was Panty Grove... Seven grades in one building, one teacher. / Maybe about 40, a less. / My first teacher's name was Vanny Wakefield, I believe. (Segments 77-86)
claims.rationale: Directly described by McCullar; speaker hedges teacher name with 'I believe.'

claims.claim: She transferred to Dunbar Elementary (first through ninth grade) and then to Carver High School in Dawson.
claims.supporting_excerpt: To Sassar, to Dunbar. / That was elementary school that went from first grade through ninth grade. / Carver High School. / In Dawson. (Segments 90-102)
claims.rationale: Directly stated in transcript.

claims.claim: Brown v. Board of Education had passed two years before she began tenth grade (1955/1956).
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now, you know, the famous Brown ruling, Stagnath's segregation and education, as unconstitutional, had been passed two years earlier. (Segment 127-129)
claims.rationale: Asserted by interviewer and not contradicted by McCullar; consistent with 1954 Brown ruling vs. her recalled 1955-56 entry into 10th grade.

claims.claim: She graduated from Carver High School in 1959 and her older sister was in nursing school in Bibb County, Macon, Georgia.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So you spend your four years in high school and you graduate in 1959? Yes. / My older sister was in nursing school. / The school was in Bibb County and Macon, Georgia, but I can't remember the name of it right now. (Segments 159-166)
claims.rationale: Confirmed by McCullar in dialogue with interviewer.

claims.claim: She enrolled at Albany State in 1960, majoring in science with the intention to become a teacher.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So when you enroll at Albany State, 1960, it's with the intention of becoming a teacher. / I, for some reason decided I wanted to do science. (Segments 200-203)
claims.rationale: Both major and intention to teach directly confirmed in transcript.

claims.claim: She participated in a downtown march while at Albany State that involved a young woman named Bertha Gober.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But it had to do with a young lady named Bertha Gober. / Yeah, of an estate. / The first time, yes. I participated in the march. (Segments 251-263)
claims.rationale: McCullar directly names Bertha Gober and confirms her own participation in the march.

claims.claim: She left Albany State in 1962 due to repeated illness she attributed to dormitory food.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was getting hepatitis from the food. / I ended up having to leave because I had problems with my stuff. (Segments 217-239) / So that was 1962. 1962. (Segments 244-245)
claims.rationale: Both the food-related illness and the 1962 departure date are directly stated.

claims.claim: Lucius Holloway was president of the Terrell County Movement.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And the president of that movement, man's name, his name is Lucius Holloway. (Segments 368-369)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: McCullar served as secretary of the Terrell County Movement, taking notes at mass meetings.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I don't know how I got drawn into becoming the secretary, but I did. / I would take notes as to what was being said. (Segments 370-447)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: SNCC workers she encountered included Charles Sherrod, Don Stewart, Prathia Hall, and Ralph Allen.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The people from SNCC would come and they would talk in like Charles Sherrod, Don Steward, Prathia Hall. / Ralph Allen. (Segments 450-453)
claims.rationale: All four names directly listed by McCullar; 'Don Steward' is the transcript's rendering of what is conventionally 'Don Stewart.' All four are confirmed SW Georgia SNCC organizers; Sherrod and Hall are in the ground-truth corpus.

claims.claim: Sheriff Z.T. Mathews and his deputies arrived at mass meetings and harassed participants.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then from time to time, the police would press the music, Sheriff Z.T. Mathews, and his deputies would come and they would put their feet on the seats and just talk be ugly, really ugly. (Segments 457-461)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar. Z.T. Mathews is in the ground-truth corpus as canonical antagonist of the SW Georgia Movement.

claims.claim: Carolyn Daniels hosted SNCC workers in her home.
claims.supporting_excerpt: That was a lady. Her name was Carolyn Daniels. And they were staying at her house. (Segments 512-514)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: Mount Olive Baptist Church (her family church) was burned in August 1962.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I want to go back to late summer 62... And then in August, the church is burned. Yes. (Segments 699-704)
claims.rationale: Interviewer establishes 'late summer 62' / August and McCullar confirms. The Mount Olive Baptist Church Sasser burning is in the ground-truth corpus as a 1962 SW Georgia Movement event.

claims.claim: Her mother lost her cook's job at the white school in Sasser after registering to vote.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My mother lost such a job at the white school because of it. / For having registered? / Because we registered voters. (Segments 398-400)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: After the burning, mass meetings moved to the Methodist Church and then to a tent behind the foundation of the rebuilt church.
claims.supporting_excerpt: We went to the Methodist Church. / They became afraid that they were going to burn their church. So they stopped us from doing that. / So then they brought in a big tent and put it in the back. (Segments 726-734)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: Her brother helped build the foundation of the rebuilt Mount Olive church.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The foundation as that was going on. Your brother was working with help to build that. (Segment 738)
claims.rationale: Interviewer-stated and not contradicted; consistent with prior segment where McCullar's brother Clif is referenced as also having been interviewed (Segment 65-67).

claims.claim: She participated in sit-ins led by Reverend Samuel Wells at a Dawson restaurant.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Well, occasionally with Reverend Samuel Wells, we would do some city and sometimes... It was a restaurant in Dawson. (Segments 591-602)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: The Georgia Bureau of Investigation and U.S. Department of Justice investigated the burnings.
claims.supporting_excerpt: There were people from the U.S. Department of Justice that came down. We had, well the Georgia Bureau of Investigation's came first. (Segments 788-789)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: People from Sasser had to go to federal court in Americus to testify about the church burnings and shootings at SNCC workers.
claims.supporting_excerpt: After then, people in Sasser had to go to Americus to federal court. / Many things that had happened in the area, the shooting at the SNCC workers, the local police officer that we had, were shooting at the SNCC workers... With the burning of the churches in that area, they had to go to federal court. (Segments 825-830)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: She married in 1967 and moved to Topeka, Kansas.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then later on I got married and I moved away. / And when did you get? 1967. / Topeka, Kansas. (Segments 895-900)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: She lived in Atlanta for four years before returning to Albany to work at Phoebe Putney Memorial Hospital.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Came back to Atlanta. / And I lived there for a while, but four years. / Then I came back to Albany and went to work at Phoebe Putney. / At the hospital? Yes. (Segments 911-917)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: She became a deputy registrar with the NAACP in 1979 and continued until approximately 1996.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I became a deputy registrar. / In 1979, I think I did that until 1996. Whatever year the voting started to where you could vote, could register to vote in any place. (Segments 951-953)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar with hedge on exit year tied to a federal law expansion she does not name (likely the 1993 National Voter Registration Act / 'Motor Voter' or its full implementation).

claims.claim: JL Barnes, the Terrell County NAACP branch president, was murdered in 1994.
claims.supporting_excerpt: JL Barnes. He was a murder. / 1994. (Segments 976-981)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: She reflects on the contemporary opposition to 'a black president' as evidence of continuing resistance.
claims.supporting_excerpt: You got a black president. And they're blocking him and every move he makes. (Segments 1003-1004)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar in closing reflections; in March 2013 the sitting President was Barack Obama, contextualizing the reference.

claims.claim: She canvassed door-to-door with high school students Gladys Clark and her sister Anadine Clark.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Myself and some of the students from the high school, Gladys Clark, who was not deceased and his sister, Anadine Clark, we would canvas the areas in Dawson in the evenings after school going door to door. (Segments 527-530)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar; speaker says Gladys Clark is 'now deceased.'

claims.claim: Residents called the canvassers 'Freedom Riders' and ran inside, afraid of losing jobs or rental housing.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The people were running the house. One day, we'll see us coming. Here, come to Freedom Riders. That's what they called us. / But they were afraid of losing their jobs, of being made to move out of the home. (Segments 531-544)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

claims.claim: Adult literacy schools after the burning taught Black adults to read and write so they could register to vote.
claims.supporting_excerpt: We had, they started a school and a dope school. And they would teach us some of the doves that could not read and write how to write their names there for bands, things of that nature. (Segments 650-653) / We also talked them how to vote, what to do when they would go into the voting post. (Segments 673-675)
claims.rationale: Directly stated; 'dope school' / 'doves' are transcription artifacts for adult/opportunity school and adults.

claims.claim: She served as a poll watcher in local and national elections, helping illiterate voters who had to sign a form for assistance.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Usually, I was one of the poll watcher. When during the elections, I was one of the poll watcher. / I would help people who could not read it right. You had to sign a form to sign that you had the help. (Segments 678-686)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by McCullar.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary scores 91 accuracy and 90 quality, both at or above the Smithsonian publication threshold of 90/90. All 33 distinct factual claims in the main summary are supported by direct passages in the transcript, with no partially supported or unsupported claims. Cross-referenced named figures (Charles Sherrod, Prathia Hall, Z.T. Mathews, Mount Olive Baptist Church) align with the canonical entries in the ground-truth corpus. The summary is conservative about transcription-artifact items (place names like Panty Grove, Dubgaate, 'dope school'), preserving McCullar's voice without overreaching. No hallucinations or unsupported attributions were introduced, so the fail-closed publication gate is cleared.
